questions.explanation: Tokenization is the preprocessing step that breaks raw text into tokens (words, subwords, or characters) for downstream modeling.

options.A: Splitting text into smaller units such as words or subwords
options.B: Translating text from one language to another
options.C: Reducing model size by pruning weights

questions.question: In NLP, what is tokenization?

questions.explanation: A set stores unique elements and automatically removes duplicates, which is useful for vocabulary or unique token collections.
questions.question: Which Python data structure is most appropriate for storing unique tokens while automatically removing duplicates?

questions.explanation: The Poisson distribution models counts of events occurring independently within a fixed interval (e.g., word occurrences in a document).
questions.question: Which probability distribution is commonly used to model the number of occurrences of a rare word in a fixed-length document?

questions.explanation: model.train() sets layers like Dropout and BatchNorm to training behavior; it does not run training by itself.

options.A: Switches the model to training mode (e.g., enables dropout, uses batchnorm statistics appropriately)

questions.question: In PyTorch, what does calling model.train() do?

questions.explanation: TF-IDF over word/n-gram features is a standard, effective baseline for many text classification tasks with linear models.

options.A: Raw character codes (ASCII values) concatenated into a vector
options.B: TF-IDF vectors over word or n-gram features
options.C: Random vectors sampled from a normal distribution
options.D: Only the document length as a single feature

questions.question: You are building a linear classifier for sentiment analysis. Which feature representation is typically most suitable as a strong baseline for text classification?
questions.topic: Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering For Text

questions.explanation: Welch’s t-test compares means of two independent samples without assuming equal variances.
questions.question: You want to compare the mean token length between two independent corpora (Corpus A vs Corpus B). Which statistical test is most appropriate if token lengths are approximately normally distributed but variances may differ?

questions.explanation: Macro-F1 weights each class equally, making it more informative than accuracy when classes are imbalanced.

options.B: Macro-averaged F1 score across entity classes

questions.question: For a highly imbalanced named-entity recognition dataset, which evaluation approach best reflects performance on the minority entity classes?
questions.topic: Model Training & Evaluation For Nlp (Metrics, Validation)

questions.explanation: Hyperparameters should be tuned using validation data (or CV) and final performance reported on a separate test set to prevent leakage.

options.A: Tune hyperparameters on the test set and report the best test score
options.B: Use a train/validation split (or cross-validation) for tuning, then evaluate once on a held-out test set
options.C: Train on the full dataset and report training accuracy
options.D: Shuffle labels during training to reduce overfitting

questions.question: You are fine-tuning a Hugging Face Transformer for text classification and want to avoid data leakage while selecting hyperparameters. Which setup is most appropriate?

questions.explanation: Constraints can reduce meaning drift for critical terms, and learned MT metrics (e.g., COMET) plus human error analysis better capture adequacy than BLEU alone.

options.A: Increase beam size indefinitely and evaluate only with BLEU
options.B: Add constrained decoding or terminology constraints and evaluate with COMET (or BLEURT) plus targeted human error analysis
options.C: Remove the attention mechanism and evaluate with token-level accuracy
options.D: Train only on monolingual target-language data and evaluate with perplexity

questions.question: A machine translation system produces fluent but meaning-altering translations (hallucinations). Which combined change is most likely to reduce this while keeping quality measurable?

questions.explanation: Avoiding shell string concatenation, using safe argument passing, validating inputs, and adding security tests are standard secure engineering practices to prevent command injection exploits.

options.A: Keep the code but add a comment warning about security risks
options.B: Replace shell invocation with a safe API or subprocess call using an argument list, validate/escape inputs, and add tests for command-injection cases
options.C: Base64-encode the user input before concatenation to the shell command
options.D: Log the full command string for debugging and deploy as-is

questions.question: During an ICT code review of an internal NLP microservice, you notice user-provided text is passed into a shell command via string concatenation. What is the best engineering response that also reduces exploitability?
